subtopic_detail: Severe acute ulcerative colitis with Truelove-Witts criteria
stem: A 28-year-old man with a 3-year history of ulcerative colitis on oral mesalazine presents with 7 days of worsening bloody diarrhoea now occurring 10 times per day with marked nocturnal symptoms. He has crampy abdominal pain and feels systemically unwell. He has no recent travel, no recent antibiotics, and is up to date with vaccinations. There is no toxic megacolon on plain abdominal X-ray and no peritonism on examination today in the ward bed area.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 110/70, HR 108, RR 18, Temp 38.1 degrees Celsius, SpO2 99% on room air
data_table.Examination: Soft abdomen with diffuse mild tenderness, no guarding, no rebound; bowel sounds present
data_table.Investigations: Hb 102 g/L, WCC 14.2 x10^9/L, platelets 480 x10^9/L, CRP 84 mg/L, albumin 28 g/L; stool C difficile PCR negative, faecal calprotectin elevated; plain abdominal X-ray no megacolon

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate initial management?

options.text: Admit for intravenous hydrocortisone 100 mg six hourly, VTE prophylaxis with enoxaparin, and stool tests including Clostridioides difficile PCR with daily reassessment of response
options.rationale: Truelove-Witts severe UC is treated with IV corticosteroids and VTE prophylaxis; CDI testing rules out the major mimic. (Source: GESA IBD guidance)

options.text: Continue oral mesalazine 4 g daily and add oral budesonide 9 mg daily with outpatient gastroenterology review in 2 weeks once symptoms have plateaued at the current intensity
options.rationale: Oral aminosalicylates and budesonide are inadequate for severe acute UC; IV steroids are required. (Source: GESA IBD guidance)

options.text: Commence empirical oral ciprofloxacin 500 mg twice daily and oral metronidazole 400 mg three times daily for 7 days to cover bacterial overgrowth and travel-related pathogens today
options.rationale: Empirical antibiotics are not first-line in acute severe UC without infection; they delay definitive immunosuppression. (Source: GESA IBD guidance)

options.text: Commence subcutaneous adalimumab 160 mg loading dose today and 80 mg in 2 weeks as the first-line biologic agent without starting any intravenous corticosteroid therapy beforehand
options.rationale: Biologics are second-line after failure or contraindication to IV corticosteroids in acute severe UC; adalimumab is not first-choice rescue. (Source: GESA IBD guidance)

options.text: Plan urgent total colectomy within 24 hours of admission regardless of corticosteroid response in this patient with acute severe ulcerative colitis presentation now today
options.rationale: Colectomy is for failure of medical rescue, toxic megacolon, perforation or massive haemorrhage; not an immediate first response. (Source: GESA IBD guidance)

explanation.summary: Acute severe ulcerative colitis (Truelove-Witts criteria) is treated by admission, IV hydrocortisone 100 mg six hourly, VTE prophylaxis (despite bleeding), CDI exclusion, and daily clinical reassessment. Day 3 Travis criteria (stool frequency above 8 or CRP above 45 mg/L with 3 to 8 stools) predict steroid failure and trigger rescue therapy with infliximab or ciclosporin and surgical review. Colectomy is reserved for refractory or complicated disease.
key_points: IV hydrocortisone 100 mg six hourly is first-line in acute severe UC
Day 3 Travis criteria identify steroid non-responders for rescue therapy
VTE prophylaxis is essential despite bloody stools because of pro-thrombotic flare state
explanation.pearls: Day 3 of IV steroids: more than 8 stools per day or CRP above 45 with 3 to 8 stools predicts steroid failure and triggers infliximab or ciclosporin rescue and surgical co-review.

subtopic_detail: Postpartum long-acting reversible contraception at 6 weeks
stem: A 28-year-old woman attends her 6-week postnatal visit. She is breastfeeding her healthy term baby on demand. She has no thromboembolic history and her BP is normotensive. She does not want another pregnancy for at least 3 years and asks about reliable contraception that does not interfere with breastfeeding. She has had no postnatal complications, normal lochia and a normal pelvic examination today. She has had her postnatal mood and EPDS screening completed.

data_table.Examination: Soft non-tender abdomen, well-healed perineum, cervix appears parous and closed
data_table.Investigations: Full blood count and ferritin within normal range; no current medications other than postnatal vitamins; baby thriving and feeding

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate first-line contraceptive recommendation?

options.text: Recommend reliance on the lactational amenorrhoea method for the next 12 months as the most effective postpartum contraceptive strategy for this woman who is currently breastfeeding her infant
options.rationale: LAM has time-limited efficacy (under 6 months, exclusive breastfeeding, amenorrhoea); it is not first-line for 3-year horizons. (Source: RANZCOG postnatal contraception statement)

options.text: Offer immediate insertion of an etonogestrel subdermal implant at this visit or a copper or progestogen-releasing intrauterine device after counselling about LARC efficacy
options.rationale: LARC methods are first-line postpartum: progestogen-only options do not affect breastfeeding and the implant or IUD can be placed at the 6-week visit. (Source: RANZCOG postnatal contraception statement)

options.text: Commence a combined oral contraceptive containing 30 micrograms ethinylestradiol and 150 micrograms levonorgestrel daily as the first-line agent for this breastfeeding woman at 6 weeks
options.rationale: Combined hormonal contraceptives are avoided while exclusively breastfeeding because they reduce milk volume; progestogen-only methods are preferred. (Source: RANZCOG postnatal contraception statement)

options.text: Recommend tubal ligation by laparoscopy at the next available theatre list as the most effective contraceptive option in this woman who would like 3 years between pregnancies
options.rationale: Permanent sterilisation is not indicated in a woman who wants further pregnancies in the future. (Source: RANZCOG postnatal contraception statement)

options.text: Suggest condom use alone for the next 6 months and reassess at her routine cervical screening visit in 4 years for a definitive longer-acting method of contraception then
options.rationale: Condoms are typical-use 18 percent failure; not first-line when LARC is acceptable. (Source: RANZCOG postnatal contraception statement)

explanation.summary: Long-acting reversible contraception is first-line postpartum: progestogen-only methods do not affect lactation and can be commenced from immediate postpartum (implant or IUD post-placental insertion) or at the 6-week visit. Combined hormonal contraceptives are avoided while exclusively breastfeeding because of breast milk volume reduction and VTE risk. Lactational amenorrhoea has efficacy only under 6 months in exclusive breastfeeding with amenorrhoea.
key_points: Progestogen-only LARC (implant, hormonal IUD) does not affect breastfeeding
Combined hormonal contraceptives are avoided while exclusively breastfeeding
Lactational amenorrhoea has time-limited efficacy under 6 months with exclusive feeding
explanation.pearls: Etonogestrel implants and the LNG-IUS have typical-use failure rates under 1 per 100 woman-years and do not affect lactation when started postpartum.

stem: A 26-year-old G1P0 at 9+1 weeks presents to the early pregnancy clinic with light fresh PV spotting for 24 hours, no clots and no abdominal pain. She is haemodynamically stable, afebrile and feels well. She has no fevers, no foul-smelling discharge and no chemical or recreational drug exposure. Her booking bloods at 8 weeks were unremarkable. A bedside transvaginal ultrasound today demonstrates a live intrauterine pregnancy with appropriate biometry and a small 1.5 cm subchorionic haematoma.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/70, HR 76, Temp 36.7 degrees Celsius, SpO2 99% on room air
data_table.Examination: Speculum: small amount of fresh blood in posterior fornix; cervical os closed; uterus soft non-tender on bimanual examination
data_table.Investigations: TVS: live intrauterine singleton pregnancy with CRL 24 mm consistent with 9 weeks 1 day; FHB 162 bpm; 1.5 cm subchorionic haematoma; serum bHCG and progesterone not yet measured

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate management plan?

options.text: Prescribe oral misoprostol 800 micrograms vaginally as a single dose to expedite resolution of the threatened miscarriage and avoid prolonged uncertainty for this woman today
options.rationale: Misoprostol is used for missed or incomplete miscarriage, not for threatened miscarriage with a live fetus; this is iatrogenic pregnancy loss. (Source: RANZCOG early pregnancy loss guideline)

options.text: Admit for bed rest for 7 to 10 days and intravenous progesterone supplementation as the routine management for threatened miscarriage at 9 weeks gestation in primary care
options.rationale: Bed rest does not reduce miscarriage risk; routine progesterone is reserved for women with prior recurrent miscarriage and current bleeding. (Source: RANZCOG early pregnancy loss guideline)

options.text: Reassure with safety-net advice, provide RhD typing if not yet done, and arrange clinic follow-up with repeat ultrasound in 1 to 2 weeks if symptoms persist or worsen
options.rationale: Threatened miscarriage with a viable pregnancy is managed expectantly with safety-net advice, RhD typing and follow-up scan. (Source: RANZCOG early pregnancy loss guideline)

options.text: Arrange immediate surgical evacuation by suction curettage as the best management option for this woman with confirmed threatened miscarriage at 9 weeks gestation today in the operating theatre
options.rationale: Surgical evacuation of a viable pregnancy is termination, not management of threatened miscarriage. (Source: RANZCOG early pregnancy loss guideline)

options.text: Administer methotrexate 50 mg per square metre intramuscularly as the standard first-line approach to threatened miscarriage with a small subchorionic haematoma today
options.rationale: Methotrexate is used for ectopic pregnancy or termination; using it for a viable pregnancy with bleeding is iatrogenic loss. (Source: AMH: methotrexate)

explanation.summary: Threatened miscarriage with a live intrauterine pregnancy is managed expectantly with safety-net advice (urgent review for heavy bleeding, severe pain, or febrile illness), RhD typing and anti-D for any sensitising bleed (above 12 weeks; under 12 weeks only for high-volume events) and a follow-up scan within 1 to 2 weeks if symptoms persist. Bed rest does not influence outcomes. Progesterone supplementation is considered only for women with recurrent miscarriage and current bleeding.
key_points: Bed rest does not change the natural history of threatened miscarriage
RhD typing and anti-D should be considered for sensitising bleeding events
Progesterone supplementation is reserved for recurrent miscarriage with current bleeding
explanation.pearls: Subchorionic haematomas under 25 percent of the gestational sac volume have a similar live birth rate to women without a haematoma; reassurance with follow-up is appropriate.

subtopic_detail: Antiepileptic switch from valproate before pregnancy
stem: A 28-year-old woman with generalised tonic-clonic seizures on sodium valproate 1500 mg daily for 6 years attends preconception clinic. Her last seizure was 2 years ago. She is keen to conceive within the next 12 months. She has no history of focal seizures, no other antiepileptic, no liver disease, no migraine and no contraindications to alternative antiepileptics. She does not drink alcohol and is on the AU pregnancy prevention program for valproate users currently.

data_table.Examination: Normal neurological examination; no focal deficits, no skin lesions suggestive of phakomatosis
data_table.Investigations: EEG 12 months ago without epileptiform activity on routine recording; LFT, FBC normal; on long-acting reversible contraception currently

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate preconception management plan?

options.text: Continue sodium valproate at the current dose and add folic acid 5 mg daily without altering the antiepileptic regimen, with monthly serum valproate monitoring during conception attempts
options.rationale: Valproate carries up to 10 percent major malformation and 30 to 40 percent neurodevelopmental disorder risk; continuation through pregnancy is contraindicated unless no alternative. (Source: AMH: valproate; AES preconception guidance)

options.text: Stop sodium valproate immediately today and conceive in the next 6 months without commencing any alternative antiepileptic, relying on her current 2-year seizure-free interval
options.rationale: Abrupt cessation risks seizure recurrence, including status; switching, not stopping, is the safer approach. (Source: AES preconception guidance)

options.text: Switch directly to topiramate 100 mg twice daily at standard dose with folic acid 0.5 mg daily as the safer alternative for generalised tonic-clonic seizures in this woman before conception
options.rationale: Topiramate is associated with oral clefts and is not the preferred switch; lamotrigine or levetiracetam are preferred. (Source: AES preconception guidance)

options.text: Gradually cross-taper sodium valproate to levetiracetam over months under neurology supervision with folic acid 5 mg daily, continue contraception until seizure-free on monotherapy, then attempt conception
options.rationale: Cross-taper to levetiracetam (or lamotrigine), confirm seizure freedom on monotherapy, add high-dose folate, and only then attempt conception. (Source: AES preconception guidance)

options.text: Add lamotrigine to her current valproate at standard maintenance dose and continue dual therapy throughout the pregnancy to minimise risk of breakthrough seizures occurring at any point
options.rationale: Polytherapy in pregnancy carries higher malformation risk than monotherapy; the goal is monotherapy with the safest agent. (Source: AES preconception guidance)

explanation.summary: Valproate in pregnancy causes up to 10 percent major malformation and 30 to 40 percent neurodevelopmental disorders, mandating switch before conception in any woman of reproductive potential when alternatives are available. Levetiracetam and lamotrigine are preferred substitutes for generalised epilepsy. The plan is cross-taper under neurology, confirm seizure freedom on monotherapy, add folic acid 5 mg daily, and only then deprescribe contraception. Monotherapy is preferred over polytherapy throughout pregnancy.
key_points: Valproate is contraindicated in women planning pregnancy whenever an alternative exists
Levetiracetam and lamotrigine are preferred substitutes for generalised epilepsy
Confirm seizure freedom on monotherapy before stopping contraception
explanation.pearls: Folic acid 5 mg daily (not 0.5 mg) is indicated for at least 3 months preconception in women on antiepileptics to reduce neural tube defect risk.

subtopic_detail: Endometriosis stepwise management after failed COCP
stem: A 30-year-old nulliparous woman has had chronic cyclical pelvic pain and dyspareunia for 4 years. She has been on continuous combined oral contraceptive for 12 months with persistent symptoms despite adherence and tolerable side effects. NSAIDs provide partial relief only. She has no current fertility plans. She has no current bowel or urinary symptoms, no haematuria or rectal bleeding, no abnormal cervical screening, and her pelvic examination is unremarkable today.

data_table.Examination: Mild tenderness in the posterior fornix; no adnexal mass or nodules palpable on bimanual examination; cervix appears normal
data_table.Investigations: Pelvic ultrasound: no endometrioma, no hydrosalpinx, no obvious deep infiltrating endometriosis; CA-125 not measured

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next management option?

options.text: Switch the combined oral contraceptive to a progestogen-only pill at standard dose and continue NSAIDs alone with planned review at 6 months in the gynaecology clinic
options.rationale: Switching from COCP to POP is unlikely to add symptom benefit after a year of failed continuous COCP; other progestogenic options or laparoscopy are preferred. (Source: RANZCOG endometriosis statement)

options.text: Refer for total abdominal hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy as the definitive treatment of suspected endometriosis in this woman with persistent symptoms at age 30
options.rationale: Hysterectomy with BSO is not appropriate for a 30-year-old nulliparous woman without fertility decision made and without confirmed advanced disease. (Source: RANZCOG endometriosis statement)

options.text: Commence empirical danazol 200 mg three times daily for 6 months to suppress endometriotic implants and reduce her cyclical pelvic pain symptoms in this woman before any laparoscopic intervention
options.rationale: Danazol has significant androgenic side effects and is rarely used now; LNG-IUS, GnRH antagonists or laparoscopy are preferred. (Source: RANZCOG endometriosis statement)

options.text: Offer the levonorgestrel intrauterine system at standard dose as a sole next intervention without considering laparoscopy for diagnostic confirmation and treatment in the same operative setting today
options.rationale: LNG-IUS is a reasonable progestogenic option but a see-and-treat laparoscopy after failed first-line medical therapy is the AU stepwise approach in this scenario. (Source: RANZCOG endometriosis statement)

options.text: Offer diagnostic laparoscopy with excision or ablation of any visible endometriotic lesions in the same operative setting, with postoperative hormonal suppression to reduce symptom recurrence
options.rationale: After failed first-line continuous COCP plus NSAIDs, see-and-treat laparoscopy with postop hormonal suppression is the AU stepwise approach. (Source: RANZCOG endometriosis statement)

explanation.summary: Endometriosis is managed in steps: NSAIDs and continuous COCP or progestogens first; if these fail after 6 to 12 months, see-and-treat laparoscopy with excision or ablation of lesions, followed by postoperative hormonal suppression to reduce recurrence. The LNG-IUS, GnRH antagonists (with add-back HRT after 6 months) and GnRH agonists are second-line medical options. Fertility-preserving conservative surgery is preferred over hysterectomy in younger women.
key_points: See-and-treat laparoscopy follows failure of first-line medical therapy
Postoperative hormonal suppression reduces symptom recurrence
Hysterectomy is reserved for refractory disease after fertility decisions are complete
explanation.pearls: Postoperative LNG-IUS or continuous progestogen after laparoscopic excision of endometriosis reduces 5-year symptom recurrence by around half compared with no maintenance therapy.
